channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: How America and Israel built vast military targeting machines
item.description: Software is supercharging the process of finding things to bomb

item.title: Why corporate lawyers always win
item.description: The world is a pedant’s paradise

item.title: Countries Agree to Historic Emergency Oil Reserve Release
item.description: IEA member states will distribute 400 million barrels of oil to ease the Iran war-caused supply crunch.

item.title: Trump’s New Cyber-First War Strategy
item.description: From Iran to Venezuela, Trump has fully embraced offensive cyberoperations.

item.title: How South Africa Is Navigating the Iran War
item.description: Pretoria’s awkward dance between Washington and Tehran.

item.title: Viktor Orban’s illiberal intellectual patronage system
item.description: The Budapest Megaphone blares populism to the world

item.title: The Major Local Election That France’s Far Right Might Just Win
item.description: A year before presidential elections, the National Rally has a chance of capturing Marseille, a historic hub of immigration.

item.title: Want to hack your body with peptides? If only the science agreed
item.description: Boosters say they will do everything from aiding strength, recovery and longevity

item.title: How Gap is trying to get its cool back
item.description: Its boss is using the Barbie formula to turn around the ailing fashion label

item.title: AI is helping expand the frontier of theoretical physics
item.description: It is blurring the line between tool and collaborator

item.title: Could special forces steal Iran’s uranium?
item.description: Swiping it would require one of the largest raids in military history

item.title: Could Zhipu Emerge as the AI Stock of 2026 Amid Wider Market Uncertainty?
item.description: Zhipu has soared on its Hong Kong debut. Now, following the launch of its GLM-5 model, the stock appears to be well-positioned to capitalize on increasingly uncertain global markets.

item.title: China’s Interests in the Israel-US War With Iran
item.description: Insights from Yoram Evron.

item.title: ‘Forcible Imposition’ of India’s National Song Triggers Resistance in the Country’s Northeast
item.description: Some stanzas of "Vande Mataram" refer to Hindu goddesses. India’s religious minorities say it goes against their monotheist beliefs.

item.title: At last, Haiti has some hope
item.description: Aggressive policing is weakening gangsters’ control

item.title: Japan Will Begin Deploying Homegrown Longer-Range Missiles as Counterstrike Strategy Takes Shape
item.description: March 31 will mark the first deployment of two new Japanese systems: the Hyper Velocity Gliding Projectile (HVGP) for island defense and the upgraded Type 12 surface-to-ship missile.

item.title: Africa Is Reimagining Climate Finance
item.description: Foreign donors – including governments, NGOs, and development agencies – have long based climate-finance decisions on their own perceptions of risk, imposing solutions that do not necessarily reflect African priorities or perspectives. But with new investment platforms, Africa is taking matters into its own hands.

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: The real meaning of the Iran war for Africa
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, writes about the Middle East crisis

item.title: Can the PPP Really Move on From Yoon Suk-yeol?
item.description: Hoping to avoid a wipe-out at the local elections, the party finally issued an apology. But it’s going to be difficult to shake its pro-Yoon image – especially with the current leadership.

item.title: How Will This Energy Shock Play Out?
item.description: Although the US-Israeli war against Iran is unprecedented in many ways, previous energy and financial crises over the past half-century offer useful lessons for investors, policymakers, and others hoping to navigate the current storm. For starters, oil and natural gas producers cannot assume that prices will stay high indefinitely.

item.title: Gaza’s Reconstruction Must Begin With Its Youth
item.description: A better future for Palestine depends on psychological recovery.

item.title: When Wang Yi Speaks, It’s Not Just China’s Foreign Minister Talking
item.description: Wang’s unprecedented consolidation of roles has made the Two Sessions press conference the most authoritative foreign policy signal Beijing produces. It has also made it the most rigid.

item.title: Asia’s Energy Triage Amid the Iran War
item.description: The conflict is exposing a hierarchy of energy vulnerability across the Indo-Pacific.

item.title: The Next Cuban Revolution Could Be Peaceful
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s effective oil blockade of Cuba has plunged the country into crisis. In such a weakened state, the Cuban regime could be motivated to launch a transition to a market-based economy if offered a chance to integrate economically with the US, Canada, and Mexico.

item.title: Ahead of Constitutional Referendum Social Media Companies Restrict Journalist Accounts in Kazakhstan
item.description: Censorship has been on the rise as the election date, March 15, approaches.

item.title: Liquefied natural gas: the overlooked economic chokepoint
item.description: Alternatives to Gulf supplies are scare

item.title: Trump Is Showing China How to Seize Taiwan
item.description: Great powers study each other closely, observing the strategies used, the resistance met, and the outcomes realized. Donald Trump's strangulation of Cuba thus could become a template for others, beginning with Xi Jinping, who remains committed to achieving "reunification" with Taiwan.

item.title: Why Chaos in Washington Can’t Stop California
item.description: Even as the Trump administration unnerves global markets with its erratic policymaking, California remains a top destination for foreign investors. Capital is flowing in because the state’s economic-policy strategy offers what the current federal government cannot: clarity and predictability.

item.title: Mojtaba’s Selection Is a Sign of Political Exhaustion
item.description: In choosing Khamenei’s son, the regime has imposed exactly what many Iranians feared.

item.title: Mojtaba’s Selection Is a Sign of Political Exhaustion
item.description: In choosing Khamenei’s son, the regime has imposed exactly what many Iranians feared.

item.title: Why Haven’t the Houthis Fired?
item.description: The rational calculus, for the moment, is that death to America can wait.

item.title: Why Haven’t the Houthis Fired?
item.description: The rational calculus, for the moment, is that death to America can wait.

item.title: Is Khamenei’s Killing Narrowing the Shia-Sunni divide in South Asia?
item.description: Arab-Iran conflicts have traditionally roiled Shia-Sunni relations in South Asia. Things are a little different this time.

item.title: The Wisdom of Europe’s “Great Capitulation”
item.description: The European Union was widely criticized for agreeing to an asymmetrical trade deal with the US last summer. But in doing so, it effectively ensured that the US would continue harming itself by imposing high costs on its own businesses and consumers, while taking the economically sound step of lowering Europe’s residual tariffs.

item.title: Iran War and India’s Diplomatic Failures
item.description: A country of India’s size and energy dependence cannot afford to place all its geopolitical bets on one side of a regional divide.

item.title: Southeast Asia and the Middle East Energy Shock
item.description: Countries across the region have banned fuel exports and are attempting to reduce fuel consumption in anticipation of a regional energy crunch.

item.title: The Curse of Middle-Sized Wars
item.description: In Iran, Trump risks falling into a familiar trap.

item.title: America’s Endangered AI
item.description: How weak cyberdefenses threaten U.S. tech dominance.

item.title: Iran’s Drone Advantage
item.description: The Pentagon copied Tehran's technology but is still struggling to keep up.

item.title: Can America clear the Strait of Hormuz of Iran’s drones and mines?
item.description: Escorting convoys of oil tanker with warships may give Iran juicy American targets

item.title: India has much to lose from a world in chaos
item.description: Not long ago, India was cocky about handling an unpredictable America. No longer

item.title: Donald Trump’s options to cool oil prices are sorely limited
item.description: He says he has a plan. It would need to be very cunning

item.title: Time to buy the most rubbish stocks you can find
item.description: The dash for trash

item.title: A new wave of disrupters takes on American health care
item.description: Patients are unhappy. Can AI help?

item.title: “Trophy wives” are out of fashion
item.description: Our analysis finds that age gaps among America’s richest couples are shrinking

item.title: Fukushima and the ‘Lessons Learned’ From Nuclear Disasters
item.description: Nuclear accidents have become opportunities to strengthen and improve nuclear safety – while conveniently forgetting the lasting harm done to the victims.

item.title: 15 Years after Fukushima: Japan’s Stalling Nuclear Revival
item.description: The underlying structural obstacles have made a genuine nuclear power revival impossible – but Japan’s government seems unaware of this reality.

item.title: Ukraine’s housing market is increasingly peculiar
item.description: With blood in the streets, many are buying property

item.title: Lost Latino love could cost Republicans the midterms
item.description: Trump’s much-vaunted success with Hispanics is turning to ashes

item.title: America’s war aims may be diverging from Israel’s
item.description: For Binyamin Netanyahu they are, as ever, a question of politics

item.title: The War Room newsletter: How the Iran war is escalating
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, explains that the conflict is intensifying in four ways
